1.pericope_label: The Holy Spirit Comes at Pentecost
1.synthesis: On the day of Pentecost the gathered believers are filled with the Holy Spirit: a sound like a rushing wind fills the house, tongues "as of fire" rest on each, and they begin to speak in other languages "as the Spirit gave them utterance" (vv.1–4). Devout Jews from every nation, in Jerusalem for the feast, are bewildered to hear "in our own tongues the mighty works of God" (vv.5–11). Amazed and perplexed, some ask "What does this mean?" while others mock, "They are filled with new wine" (vv.12–13). The promise of Acts 1:5 is fulfilled, and the church is born in power.
1.historical_context: Pentecost (Shavuot, the Feast of Weeks) was one of the three pilgrim festivals requiring attendance at Jerusalem (Deuteronomy 16:16), so the city teemed with diaspora Jews; Luke's catalogue of nations from Parthia to Rome (vv.9–11) spans the known world and mirrors the Table of Nations (Genesis 10). The "rushing wind" and "fire" (vv.2–3) are theophany imagery from Sinai, fitting since Shavuot commemorated the giving of the Law; the Spirit is the wind (rûaḥ) that breathes life (cf. Ezekiel 37:9). The tongues are recognizable foreign languages (v.6), and Jewish tradition that God's voice at Sinai went out in all the languages of the nations sharpens the reversal: where Babel scattered tongues (Genesis 11), Pentecost gathers them.
1.literary_structure: This scene is the pivot of Luke-Acts, the empowering that makes the worldwide witness of Acts 1:8 possible, and it is deliberately framed as a new Sinai and an un-Babel. Luke describes the phenomena cautiously — a sound "like" wind, tongues "as of" fire (vv.2–3) — language straining at a reality beyond it. The list of nations (vv.9–11) anticipates the gospel's reach to all peoples, and the divided crowd — some amazed, some mocking (vv.12–13) — sets up Peter's sermon, which the charge of drunkenness directly occasions (Acts 2:15).
1.theological_application: Pentecost is the gift that turns frightened followers into bold witnesses: the Spirit's coming is power for mission, not merely private experience (v.4). That the "mighty works of God" are heard by each in his own language (v.11) signals that the gospel will cross every cultural and linguistic barrier, reaching people where they are. The reversal of Babel is good news for a fractured world: what human pride scattered, the Spirit gathers into one worshiping people. And the divided response — wonder and scorn (vv.12–13) — reminds the church that even God's plainest works will be variously received.
1.christology: The outpoured Spirit is the gift of the exalted Christ: what is seen and heard at Pentecost is the visible evidence that Jesus has ascended to the Father's right hand and "poured out" the Spirit (cf. Acts 2:33). The new Sinai imagery presents Jesus as the giver of a greater law — the Spirit written on hearts (Jeremiah 31:33) — surpassing Moses. The undoing of Babel through the proclamation of God's mighty works (v.11) shows that the risen Lord is gathering the scattered nations into the one people of God that the prophets foresaw.

14.synthesis: Peter stands with the Eleven and interprets the moment by Scripture. This is not drunkenness but the last-days outpouring Joel foretold: "I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh… everyone who calls on the name of the Lord shall be saved" (vv.16–21). He then proclaims Jesus of Nazareth, "delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God" and crucified, whom "God raised up, loosing the pangs of death" (vv.22–24). He proves the resurrection from David's words in Psalms 16 — the Holy One who would not see corruption (vv.25–31) — and Jesus' exaltation from Psalms 110 (vv.34–35), reaching the verdict: "God has made him both Lord and Christ, this Jesus whom you crucified" (v.36).
14.historical_context: Peter's speech (vv.14–36) is the most carefully built sermon in Acts, structured around scriptural citation: Joel's prophecy (Joel 2:28–32), eyewitness testimony, and the proof-texts of Psalms 16:8–11 and Psalms 110:1. His "this is what was spoken" (v.16) follows the pesher method known from Qumran — declaring a prophetic text fulfilled in a present event. Joel's universal sweep, "all flesh," with sons and daughters, young and old, male and female servants (vv.17–18), dismantles the old social limits on prophetic reception. Crucially, Joel's "name of the Lord" (YHWH) on whom one must call to be saved (v.21) is applied by Peter to Jesus (cf. Acts 2:38), an implicit confession of his deity.
14.literary_structure: The sermon moves from phenomenon to Scripture to Christ: it explains the Spirit (Joel), then narrates and interprets Jesus' death and resurrection, and climaxes in the title-laden conclusion of v.36. Its argumentative spine is the contrast between David and his greater Son: David died, was buried, and "saw corruption," so Psalms 16:10 must speak of the One who did not — the risen Jesus (vv.29–31) — and David did not ascend, so Psalms 110:1's enthronement belongs to Christ (v.34). The repeated appeal "of that we all are witnesses" (v.32) grounds the proclamation in eyewitness testimony, the criterion set in Acts 1:22.
14.theological_application: Peter holds together two truths the gospel never separates: Jesus was killed by lawless hands and "according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God" (v.23) — human guilt and divine sovereignty affirmed in one breath, not a tension to resolve but the shape of the cross. The promise that "everyone who calls on the name of the Lord shall be saved" (v.21) opens salvation freely to all who turn to Jesus, the heart of apostolic preaching. And the sermon models proclamation that is saturated with Scripture and centered on the death and resurrection of Christ.
14.christology: The sermon's verdict is among the New Testament's boldest: "God has made him both Lord and Christ" (v.36) — the crucified Jesus is the Messiah and the Kyrios, the very Lord of Psalms 110:1 enthroned at God's right hand. By applying to Jesus both the divine name on which sinners call (v.21) and the resurrection promised to God's "Holy One" (v.27), Peter confesses his divine identity and his identity as the true Son of David. The resurrection is God's vindication and exaltation of the One the world condemned: he reigns now, and from his throne pours out the Spirit (v.33).

37.synthesis: The word pierces the hearers: "cut to the heart," they cry, "Brothers, what shall we do?" (v.37). Peter answers with the gospel's first invitation: "Repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins, and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit" — for the promise is "for you and for your children and for all who are far off" (vv.38–39). With many more words he urges them to "save yourselves from this crooked generation," and that day about three thousand are baptized and added to the church (vv.40–41). The Spirit-empowered preaching of the risen Christ bears its first great harvest.
37.historical_context: To be "cut to the heart" (v.37) is the conviction that the same crowd implicated in the crucifixion (Acts 2:36) now feels under the word. Peter's call to "repent and be baptized" (v.38) draws on the Baptist's baptism of repentance (Luke 3:3) but now "in the name of Jesus Christ," marking allegiance to him and forgiveness through him. The promise "for all who are far off" (v.39) echoes the language of Gentile inclusion (Isaiah 57:19; cf. Ephesians 2:13), quietly anticipating the mission to the nations. The three thousand baptized in one day (v.41) was feasible given Jerusalem's many ritual immersion pools used by Passover and Pentecost pilgrims.
37.literary_structure: The crowd's question "What shall we do?" (v.37) supplies the hinge from sermon to response, and Peter's reply gives Acts its paradigm of conversion — repentance, baptism in Jesus' name, forgiveness, and the gift of the Spirit (v.38) — a pattern echoed and varied throughout the book. The widening scope "for you… your children… all who are far off" (v.39) traces the same expanding circles as Acts 1:8. The summary number (v.41) is the first of Luke's recurring growth-notices that punctuate Acts (cf. Acts 6:7), tracking the unstoppable advance of the word.
37.theological_application: Conviction of sin is the Spirit's work and the doorway to grace: those "cut to the heart" are not driven to despair but invited to repentance, forgiveness, and the Spirit (v.38). The gospel call is wonderfully wide — the promise reaches children and the far-off alike, "everyone whom the Lord our God calls" (v.39) — so no one need think themselves beyond its scope. And true response is decisive and public: they "received his word" and were baptized (v.41), joining the visible community of the saved.
37.christology: Forgiveness and the Spirit are now dispensed "in the name of Jesus Christ" (v.38): the exalted Lord is the one through whom sins are pardoned and the Spirit given, the saving name on which sinners call (Acts 2:21). The harvest of three thousand (v.41) is the risen Christ continuing to "do and teach" (Acts 1:1) through his witnesses — building his church by his Spirit, as he promised. The crucified One the crowd rejected is now the source of the very salvation they receive.

42.synthesis: Luke closes the chapter with a portrait of the new community: "They devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching and the fellowship, to the breaking of bread and the prayers" (v.42). Awe falls on everyone as wonders are done through the apostles; the believers share their possessions, selling goods to meet one another's needs (vv.43–45). Day by day they worship in the temple and break bread in their homes "with glad and generous hearts," praising God and enjoying the favor of all the people, "and the Lord added to their number day by day those who were being saved" (vv.46–47). The harvest of Pentecost becomes a shared life.
42.historical_context: The fourfold devotion (v.42) reflects the rhythms of early Jewish-Christian life: "the apostles' teaching" (the eyewitness instruction), "the fellowship" (koinōnia, shared life and goods), "the breaking of bread" (common meals likely including the Lord's Supper), and "the prayers" (both temple hours and gathered prayer). The community still worships "in the temple" (v.46), showing the first believers as devout Jews who saw the gospel as Israel's fulfillment, while also meeting "house to house." The voluntary sharing of possessions (vv.44–45) echoes the ideal that there be "no poor among you" (Deuteronomy 15:4) and contrasts with later failures (Acts 5:1–11).
42.literary_structure: This is the first of Luke's idealized summary panels (cf. Acts 4:32–35; Acts 5:12–16) that pause the narrative to depict the church's character. It rounds off the Pentecost account by showing what the three thousand were "added" to (Acts 2:41) — not a mere event but an ongoing common life. The closing note that "the Lord added to their number day by day" (v.47) names the true agent of growth and links back to the book's program of expansion, presenting the healthy community itself as part of the church's witness.
42.theological_application: This passage is a lasting template for the church: a life built on apostolic teaching, fellowship, the Lord's table, and prayer (v.42). The believers' open-handed generosity (v.45) shows that genuine conversion reshapes how disciples hold possessions, putting love of neighbor above private accumulation. Their joy, worship, and "favor with all the people" (vv.46–47) reveal that an authentic community is itself evangelistic — and the reminder that the Lord adds to the church guards against the conceit that growth is our achievement.
42.christology: It is "the Lord" who adds daily to the church those being saved (v.47): the risen Christ continues to build his people, the living head over the body. "The breaking of bread" (v.42) centers the community's life on him and his death, the meal in which the church remembers and communes with its Lord. The shared life of these first believers is the fruit of Pentecost and thus of Christ's exaltation — the reign of the ascended Jesus made visible in a generous, worshiping, growing community.
